coredata.dc:title: Holistic modeling of the electrolyte filling process to study the dosing and wetting behavior of lithium-ion batteries
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:issueName: 17th CIRP Conference on Intelligent Computation in Manufacturing Engineering (CIRP ICME ‘23)
coredata.dc:creator: Hagemeister, Jan
Elkhoshet, Ahmed
Hu, Yiping
Daub, Rüdiger
coredata.dc:description: The electrolyte filling process of lithium-ion batteries is characterized by extensive wetting times and significant costs for experimental process design, which is becoming increasingly relevant in the current landscape of fast-evolving cell designs to optimize energy density. In this study, an approach for applying computational fluid dynamics to simulate the flow of liquid electrolyte through the battery void volume and porous cell stack is shown. Results of the simulation on pore scale are used to calculate the permeability of the cell stack, which provides an input parameter for the simulation on cell scale. On a cell scale, the calculated parameters act as a resistance value of the cell stack, enabling the modeling of the interaction between dosing and wetting. The detailed model setup including simulation parameters is provided, so that cell manufacturers and researchers will be able to set up individual simulation models to predict the filling behavior during cell development.
coredata.dcterms:subject: lithium-ion battery cells
battery production
electrolyte filling
computational fluid dynamics
CFD
simulation

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge the support from the German Federal Ministry of Education and Research (BMBF) for their financial backing. This work stems from research performed within the scope of the projects Cell-Fill (Grant number 03XP0237B) and Sim4Pro (Grant number 03XP0242B).

xocs:doc.xocs:rawtext: ScienceDirect Available online at www.sciencedirect.com Procedia CIRP 126 (2024) 841â€“846 2212-8271 Â© 2024 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V. This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0) Peer-review under responsibility of the scientific committee of the 17th CIRP Conference on Intelligent Computation in Manufacturing Engineering (CIRP ICMEâ€˜23) 10.1016/j.procir.2024.08.268 Keywords: Lithium-ion battery cells; Battery production; Electrolyte filling; Computational fluid dynamics; CFD; Simulation 1. Introduction The expanding use of fossil fuels to meet demands for industrial growth and digital transformation has led to undeniable climate change and an increase in global average temperature [1]. It is necessary to develop innovative solutions in order to store renewable energy effectively to help counteract climate change [2]. One solution is the use of electrochemical energy storage systems such as lithium-ion batteries (LIB), which are being developed to power an ever-widening range of applications, including the electrification of transportation [3] and grid-scale energy storage [4]. The production of LIB consists of a series of complex process steps with interdepending parameters, each impacting the final cell quality [5]. First, the electrodes are produced by mixing and coating the slurry (electrode active material mixed with polymer binders and additives) on substrate foils, which is followed by the calendaring process to set the electrode performance properties. Then, the electrodes are cut to size and assembled into a cell stack or jelly roll, before the current collectors are welded together and onto the electrical terminals of the cell. Next, the cell is packaged into the cell housing (pouch, prismatic or cylindrical) and a liquid electrolyte is dosed into the cell. the latter is required for the transfer of lithium ions as charge carriers between the electrodes during cell operation. The three main components of the LIB are the anode, cathode and separator, the pores of which must be completely wetted with electrolyte before charging the cell for the first time [6] to avoid inducing safety issues such as lithium plating [7]. The wetting process can take several days depending on the cell format, making the filling and wetting processes one of the bottlenecks of battery production [8]. With the trend towards decreasing void volumes and porosities in order to improve energy densities, the filling 17th CIRP Conference on Intelligent Computation in Manufacturing Engineering Holistic modeling of the electrolyte filling process to study the dosing and wetting behavior of lithium-ion batteries Jan Hagemeistera,*, Ahmed Elkhosheta, Yiping Hua, RÃ¼diger Dauba aTechnical University of Munich, TUM School of Engineering and Design, Institute for Machine Tools and Industrial Management (iwb), BoltzmannstraÃŸe 15, 85748 Garching, Germany * Corresponding author. Tel.: +49 089 289 15469; fax: +49 089 289 15555. E-mail address: jan.hagemeister@iwb.tum.de Abstract The electrolyte filling process of lithium-ion batteries is characterized by extensive wetting times and significant costs for experimental process design, which is becoming increasingly relevant in the current landscape of fast-evolving cell designs to optimize energy density. In this study, an approach for applying computational fluid dynamics to simulate the flow of liquid electrolyte through the battery void volume and porous cell stack is shown. Results of the simulation on pore scale are used to calculate the permeability of the cell stack, which provides an input parameter for the simulation on cell scale. On a cell scale, the calculated parameters act as a resistance value of the cell stack, enabling the modeling of the interaction between dosing and wetting. The detailed model setup including simulation parameters is provided, so that cell manufacturers and researchers will be able to set up individual simulation models to predict the filling behavior during cell development. Â© 2024 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V. This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0) Peer-review under responsibility of the scientific committee of the 17th CIRP Conference on Intelligent Computation in Manufacturing Engineering (CIRP ICMEâ€˜23) 842 Jan Hagemeister et al. / Procedia CIRP 126 (2024) 841â€“846 process continues to become more challenging since more dosing steps are required and the wetting times increase. Additionally, in the fast-moving landscape of evolving cell designs and material systems, the time and cost-intensive procedure of experimental process design is a burden for cell manufacturers. For this reason, the demand for modeling the filling process to predict the wetting behavior is high, with various approaches attempting to provide an adequate solution. Knoche et al. proposed a process model to analyze cause-and- effect relationships between different materials and process parameters of the electrolyte filling step [9]. Other approaches suggest modeling the wetting analytically through the capillary forces acting on the porous LIB components [10,11]. This method has even been used to model the effect of filling process parameters [12] and gas entrapments in the separator [13]. Another approach to model the interaction of the liquid electrolyte with the gas residues in the pores is by using numerical methods such as computational fluid dynamics (CFD). Lee et al. first described the application of the lattice Boltzmann method (LBM) [14], which was later used to study the impact of wetting on the electrochemical performance of the cell [15]. Initial studies purely considered laminar flow through a porous medium [16,17] before applying the model setup to a battery application [18]. Most recently, Shodiev et al. applied the LMB to offer direct insights into the effect of structural parameters such as porosity and percentage of active material on the wetting characteristics [19]. An approach to model the filling process using commercially available CFD software was first proposed to individually model the wetting and dosing [20]. So far, no approach has been shown to holistically model the wetting of an entire cell, while considering the interaction between the dosing and wetting sub-processes. This work proposes combining the simulations of the wetting on the pore level and dosing on the cell level, to provide a meta-model of the entire electrolyte filling process. The goal is to completely describe the model setup so that manufacturers and research groups can model their individual material systems and cell geometries to study the filling process with less time and cost- intensive experiments. 2. Approach to model the electrolyte filling process 2.1. Overview To holistically model the filling process, a series of eight steps need to be performed as described in figure 1. First, a three-dimensional (3D) structure of the electrode pores was generated using either an X-ray nano-computed tomography (nano-CT) scan or a digital rendering. Then, a simulation of the electrolyte flow on a pore scale was performed. Using results for mass flow or pressure development, for example, the permeability of the pore volume was calculated. This value is then transferred to the model of the entire cell, representing the resistance of the pore volume. A resistance value was used to model the pores of the cell, since modeling each pore individually would require substantial computational power. Next, the simulation on a cell level was performed, demonstrating the interaction between dosing and wetting. Data for electrolyte in the pore and void volume is analyzed and used to provide process recommendations. A detailed description of each of the eight steps is provided in chapter 3. 2.2. Simulation parameters All simulations were performed with ANSYS Fluent version 2021R2 on a single workstation with 16 cores. In all simulations, a transient, i.e. time-dependent flow, was examined. An overview of the simulation parameters used for both the micro- and macrosimulations is provided in table A1 in the appendix. Specific parameters, such as time step size, are discussed in the respective sub-sections, since the model setup represents a key result of this work. Material parameters for the liquid electrolyte and gaseous nitrogen initially in the pores are based on literature and were chosen based on a temperature of 25 Â°C [20]. The boundary conditions for the individual models have been previously shown by Hagemeister et al. [20]. Figure 1. Overall setup of the electrolyte filling model, with the arrows demonstrating the work flow to combine a pore simulation with a large format prismatic cell scale simulation in order to model the entire filling process. Jan Hagemeister et al. / Procedia CIRP 126 (2024) 841â€“846 843 3. Results and Discussion 3.1. Geometry of the pore structure The cell stack of a LIB consists of the anode, the separator and the cathode, with each component demonstrating a unique microstructure. In the first step of the holistic model, a digital twin of the porous structure must first be acquired. Ideally, a nano-CT is performed of the cell stack components, so that each component of the cell stack is considered. An example of this is shown in the first frame of figure 1 according to Lu et al. [21]. Another option is to use 3D-generated models of the cell components that can be imported into a computer-aided design (CAD) environment in order to prepare the geometry for CFD modeling. The proposed approach applies a method for modeling the cathode structure as published by Ventura Silva et al. [22]. Since the anode and cathode show similar wetting speeds [15,23] the cathode structure is used to represent the porous structure, but the same method can be applied to model multi-material systems. First, the 3D structure was imported using the CAD software SolidWorks. Since the geometry featured spherical particles on the edges which would skew the flow results, the geometry was segmented to overall dimensions of 21 Î¼m x 98 Î¼m x 11 Î¼m. The dimension of the segmentation was chosen based on studies to maximize the volume while maintaining reasonable simulation times. Next, the inverse of the particle volume was generated such that the geometry only contains the void pore volume. The geometry was then imported into ANSYS Workbench, where the meshing was performed. Before beginning the simulation, the Reynolds number of the structure was calculated manually to be far below one, meaning that the viscous forces outweigh the inertial forces [18]. Therefore, laminar flow is assumed, resulting in the capillary action as the main physical effect during wetting. For the displacement process of the nitrogen gas in the pores, approximately incompressibility is assumed for both fluids [16]. 3.2. Microsimulation The simulation of the electrolyte flow through the microstructure was performed using the simulation parameters in table A1 of the appendix. The mesh contained approximately 600,000 elements, which was simulated with a time step size of 8.2 x 10-7 seconds. Shown in figure 2 are the results of the microsimulations, which show the electrolyte in red moving through the pore volume. The electrolyte first moves through the regions that are less densely packed, as shown in the progression between figure 2 (a) and figure 2 (b). Over time, the electrolyte continues to infiltrate the pores, beginning with the larger void areas. In figure 2 (e), most of the structure has already been filled, with regions in the middle still unwetted. The electrolyte continues to infiltrate the pores, with the blue regions showing the remaining gas in the pores leaving the structure. The boundary conditions [20] are set accordingly so that the gas can escape through the outlet, modeling the wetting behavior. Finally, figure 2 (h) shows the pore structure almost entirely filled with electrolyte as the simulation reaches a steady state. A small gas entrapment remains, as shown by the blue region, which is not unusual for the wetting of a porous structure. 3.3. Analysis of the microsimulation The goal of the microsimulation is to characterize the resistance of the pore structure. To do so, the qualitative results of the microsimulations such as the electrolyte velocity and pressure development were analyzed as shown in figure 3. The electrolyte flow velocity on the left axis shows a large initial increase caused by the electrolyte filling the available pore space. The flow speed decreases as the volume fills with electrolyte, before reaching a steady state. The interior average pressure is shown on the right axis, which demonstrates a steady increase over time as expected in defined and limited volume. Figure 2. Visualization of the simulation results of electrolyte (red) flowing through the porous structure after (a) 0 seconds, (b) 1.3 x 10-5 seconds, (c) 2.7 x 10-5 seconds, (d) 3.9 x 10-5 seconds, (e) 5.2 x 10-5 seconds, (f) 6.6 x 10-5 seconds, (g) 7.9 x 10-5 seconds and (h) 9.4 x 10-5 seconds. 844 Jan Hagemeister et al. / Procedia CIRP 126 (2024) 841â€“846 3.4. Calculation of permeability Using the data for flow velocity and interior pressure from the microsimulations, the permeability of the pore structure, k, under Darcyâ€™s Law was calculated according to equation (1) ð�‘˜ð�‘˜ = ð�‘„ð�‘„âˆ™Âµâˆ™ ð��¿ð��¿ ð��´ð��´ âˆ™ð�›¥ð�›¥ð�‘�ð�‘� (1) where Q is the electrolyte volume flow, Âµ the dynamic viscosity of the electrolyte, L is the length, A is the cross- sectional area and ð�›¥ð�›¥ð�‘�ð�‘� is the pressure difference calculated by (ð�‘�ð�‘�ð�‘–ð�‘– âˆ’ ð�‘�ð�‘�ð�‘œð�‘œ). Values for Q and ð�›¥ð�›¥ð�‘�ð�‘� are taken from the simulation results as shown in figure 3, while L and A are calculated based on the geometrical dimensions (h and w) shown in figure 4. The positions at which the pressure and flow are calculated are defined within ANSYS fluent and provided in the output file of the simulation. Shown in figure 4 is a detailed view of the porous structure as well as the electrolyte flow lines of the CFD simulation. The final value for the permeability as well as calculations such as the porosity were then transferred to the simulation on cell level. 3.5. Simulation setup on cell level Similar to the simulation of the pore structure, the simulation of the wetting on the cell level begins by importing the CAD geometry of the cell into ANSYS workbench. In this study, a cell with the format nomination of PHEV1 was used according to DIN91252 [24]. The modeled cell contains a flat wound jelly roll, which means that electrolyte can only enter the cell stack from the left and right side since the current collector foils inhibit the flow from the top and bottom. Since the porous cell internals and the cell void volume exhibit different flow properties, the two regions are defined separately. The void volume surrounding the cell stack acts as a reservoir in which the electrolyte can flow freely. Modeling each pore individually would require very high computational power and thus limit the accessibility for end users, which is why the resistance of the cell internals were defined with parameters via the ANSYS porous media model [25]. The input for the ANSYS porous media model was a porosity of 30 % and the inverse of the absolute permeability, which was calculated from the microsimulation based on the cathode volume element. The permeability value used for the simula- tion was 2 x 1014 1/m2. Fixed time steps were used with a time step size of 0.02 seconds for a total of 360,000 time-steps. 3.6. Macrosimulation The results of the macrosimulation were processed using the software CFD-post in ANSYS, as shown in figure 5. Similar to the simulation of the pore structure, the red color shows the volume fraction of the electrolyte in the various regions of the cell during the dosing and wetting stages of the filling process. The process begins with the dosing of electrolyte, as shown in figure 5 (a). The electrolyte first fills the void volume surrounding the jelly roll positioned in the center, as shown in frame (b). In frame (c), which is nine minutes after the first dosing step, the electrolyte can be seen slowly entering the porous region in the center. This process continues as shown in frame (d). Due to the limited void volume in the cell, two dosing steps are required to fill the entire electrolyte volume into the cell. Figure 5 (e) shows the second dosing step, which is evident by the electrolyte in the dosing inlet at the top of the cell. The completion of the second dosing is shown in frame (f), which is evident by the increase in electrolyte in the void volume compared to the previous frame. The wetting progresses as shown in frames (g) and (h), which is evident by the electrolyte fill level in the void volume decreasing and electrolyte volume fraction in the center of the cell increasing. After a total wetting time of 120 minutes, a wetting degree of 95 % is reached. Small gas entrapments remain in the center of the cell due to the balance of capillary and pressure-based forces. This behavior is consistent with qualitative filling experiments of the same cell format [26â€“28], showing the mostly symmetrical wetting from the outside towards the center of jelly roll inside the cell. Figure 3. Progression of the electrolyte velocity the interior pressure development as the electrolyte fills the porous structure as calculated by the microsimulations. Figure 4. Streamlines of the simulated electrolyte flow, including the parameters used to calculate the permeability. Jan Hagemeister et al. / Procedia CIRP 126 (2024) 841â€“846 845 3.7. Analysis of the macrosimulation In addition to a qualitative analysis of the wetting behavior, results from the output files are presented quantitatively. Output files provide information regarding the electrolyte volume fraction in the various regions of the cell, the pressure development and the mass flow. Monitors for each of these values must be set up before starting the simulation, as they cannot be generated retrospectively. Shown in figure 6 is the temporal course of the electrolyte volume fraction in the cellâ€™s pore volume and cell void volume. Shown on the left y-axis is the wetting degree as a percentage of the total inner volume, while the right y-axis shows the volumetric amount of electrolyte in the void volume. The wetting progresses quicker at first since the pressure force driving the wetting between the void volume and porous jelly roll is greatest. Additionally, most of the pores are still empty, allowing for an easier penetration by the electrolyte. Over time, the wetting rate decreases, before reaching a steady state. The cell is filled using two dosing steps as described in section 3.6, which are labeled in figure 6 and become evident by the rapid increase in electrolyte in the void volume. Overall, the qualitative data demonstrates the interaction between the dosing and wetting of the cell, as well as providing the overall wetting degree on the cell level. 3.8. Validation and Analysis In the final step, the model was experimentally validated for a single set of process parameters as shown by the dotted line in figure 6. The data shows a qualitative agreement between simulation and experiments [12] by the asymptotic behavior of the wetting degree â€“ the gradient of the wetting degree decreases over time and converges against 95 %. Additionally, the overall wetting times of the simulation and experiment are nearly identical after 120 minutes, reaching a wetting degree of over 95 %. Using the simulation, further studies can be performed with various process and cell parameters to provide recommendations for improving wetting times. 4. Conclusion Modeling the electrolyte filling process can help to accelerate and improve both process and cell design, especially as cell formats and material systems are evolving to improve overall energy densities. The proposed method demonstrates how the pore and cell scales can be combined to provide a holistic model of the filling process. Beginning with the pore structure, simulations are performed which are used to calculate the permeability. The results of which are used as a resistance value on the cell scale, enabling the simulation of dosing and wetting in a single simulation method. The method including details such as the simulation parameters is described in detail so that the experiments can be recreated for any material or cell system by other researchers or practitioners. Figure 6. Results of the macrosimulation, showing the progression of the simulated (Sim.) and experimental (Exp.) electrolyte wetting degree (left y-axis) as well as the simulated electrolyte remaining in the void volume (right y- axis), demonstrating the two dosing steps. 1st dosing 2nd dosing Figure 5. Simulation of the electrolyte flow in red through the void volume and jelly roll of the cell over 120 minutes, demonstrating the interaction between the dosing and wetting sub-processes. Frames are shown after (a) 0.1 seconds, (b) 2.3 seconds, (c) 9 minutes, (d) 30 minutes, (e) 30 minutes and one second, (f) 32 minutes, (g) 65 minutes and (h) 160 minutes. 846 Jan Hagemeister et al. / Procedia CIRP 126 (2024) 841â€“846 Acknowledgements The authors gratefully acknowledge the support from the German Federal Ministry of Education and Research (BMBF) for their financial backing. This work stems from research performed within the scope of the projects Cell-Fill (Grant number 03XP0237B) and Sim4Pro (Grant number 03XP0242B). Appendix Table A1. Simulation parameters for both the micro and macro simulations Setup Parameter Setting Unit General Solver type Pressure-Based - Time Transient - Gravity -9.81 m/s2 Model type Homogeneous Model Volume of Fluid - Number of Phases 2 - Viscous Model Laminar - Velocity inlet Reference frame Absolute - Velocity magnitude 6 m/s Initial gauge pressure 30000 Pa X-component of Flow 0 - Y-component of Flow -1 - Z-component of Flow 0 - Solution Method Scheme PISO - Gradient Least Squares - Pressure solver PRESTO! - Density Second Order - Momentum Second Order - References [1] J. Reisenweber, A. Gottschalk, M. Schulz, T. Knoche, G. Reinhart, M. Masuch, J. Franke, R. Reinhart, Influence of the Cell Format on the Electrolyte Filling Process of Lithiumâ€�Ion Cells, Energy Technol. 8 (2019) 1801108. https://doi.org/10.1002/ente.201801108. [27] W.J. Weydanz, H. Reisenweber, A. G

head.ce:title: Holistic modeling of the electrolyte filling process to study the dosing and wetting behavior of lithium-ion batteries

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Technical University of Munich, TUM School of Engineering and Design, Institute for Machine Tools and Industrial Management (iwb), Boltzmannstraße 15, 85748 Garching, Germany

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: TUM School of Engineering and Design
Institute for Machine Tools and Industrial Management (iwb)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aTechnical University of Munich, TUM School of Engineering and Design, Institute for Machine Tools and Industrial Management (iwb), Boltzmannstraße 15, 85748 Garching, Germany

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The electrolyte filling process of lithium-ion batteries is characterized by extensive wetting times and significant costs for experimental process design, which is becoming increasingly relevant in the current landscape of fast-evolving cell designs to optimize energy density. In this study, an approach for applying computational fluid dynamics to simulate the flow of liquid electrolyte through the battery void volume and porous cell stack is shown. Results of the simulation on pore scale are used to calculate the permeability of the cell stack, which provides an input parameter for the simulation on cell scale. On a cell scale, the calculated parameters act as a resistance value of the cell stack, enabling the modeling of the interaction between dosing and wetting. The detailed model setup including simulation parameters is provided, so that cell manufacturers and researchers will be able to set up individual simulation models to predict the filling behavior during cell development.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A new scenario logic for the Paris Agreement long-term temperature goal
Nature

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Rogelj, D. Huppmann, V. Krey, K. Riahi, L. Clarke, M. Gidden, Z. Nicholls, M. Meinshausen, A new scenario logic for the Paris Agreement long-term temperature goal, Nature 573 (2019) 357–363. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41586-019-1541-4.
